zones.name: CLI Entrypoint Shims
zones.description: Minimal binary shim files that delegate execution to the compiled CLI distributions of the three n-dx tools.
insights: Isolated files — no import edges between 3 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Zero cohesion is structurally expected — the three shim files are independent entry points with no shared imports between them, so the metric correctly reflects architecture rather than fragility
These files should remain as thin delegation wrappers; any shared CLI logic belongs in the package CLIs they invoke, not in the shim layer
At 3 files, this zone is below the 5-file reliability threshold for cohesion metrics — the zero value is structurally meaningful, not a warning signal
Cohesion of 0 is expected and correct — the three shim files (hench.js, rex.js, sourcevision.js) are parallel entry points with no shared logic, so zero intra-zone imports is the correct design.

Zero coupling confirms the bin layer has no upstream runtime dependencies, making it a clean and stable delegation boundary — changes to package CLIs do not require shim updates unless the binary path changes.

These shims enable the ndx, rex, hench, and sourcevision CLI aliases — any structural change here should be validated against the full set of command aliases documented in CLAUDE.md to avoid breaking the sv alias.

zones.name: Crash Recovery UI
zones.description: Self-contained crash detection and recovery subsystem providing a crash detector service, recovery banner component, and a hook for the viewer to surface and dismiss crash notifications.

The bidirectional import relationship with web-viewer (2 imports each direction) is architecturally documented; the direct web-shared bypass (crash → web-shared without going through web-viewer) is a documented exception that must remain the only such bypass.
crash-detector-test-support.ts is correctly placed in tests/helpers rather than src/, cleanly separating test infrastructure from the production crash zone.
Zone "crash-recovery-ui" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Bidirectional imports between crash and web-viewer (2 each direction) are documented in CLAUDE.md as an intentional architectural pattern; the direct crash→web-shared bypass must remain the sole exception to the viewer gateway rule.

Cohesion at 0.6 sits just above the dual-fragility threshold (0.5); CLAUDE.md designates this zone for proactive governance — apply the two-consumer rule to any new additions before cohesion can degrade below the threshold.
crash-detector-test-support.ts in tests/helpers is a good pattern for test infrastructure isolation; it keeps the production crash zone boundary clean while still providing shared test utilities.

The rex-e2e zone exemplifies the correct e2e test isolation pattern: zero coupling through subprocess invocation of the CLI binary, keeping tests honest about the public contract without leaking into internal imports.

The main sourcevision-core zone (163 files, cohesion 1.0, zero coupling) demonstrates ideal domain encapsulation — consumed only through domain-gateway.ts, precisely matching the intended architectural pattern.

All four fixture zones (user, json, router, sourcevision-2) have cohesion 1 and coupling 0, confirming they are correctly isolated and will not produce false edges in sourcevision's own analysis output when the tool analyzes itself.

The viewer-message-pipeline zone is the only production zone in this batch; its messaging barrel (index.ts) enforces a clean public interface that aligns with the gateway pattern required by CLAUDE.md.

Three of the fixture zones should be merged into the existing Go Analysis Test Fixtures zone — their split is a community-detection artifact of small, disconnected subgraphs rather than a meaningful architectural boundary.

web-viewer's 88-import edge to web-server is the dominant coupling surface in the web package and should be audited periodically to confirm all imports remain type-only or gateway re-exports — a single runtime import promotion could create a hard dependency between the Preact build and the Express server.

mcp-parallel-mode (web-unit) is the only dual-fragility zone in this batch; absorbing its single production file into web-server and regrouping its tests alongside other web-server tests would eliminate the artificial zone boundary entirely.

The bidirectional crash ↔ web-viewer relationship is the only documented circular zone edge in the web package; the CLAUDE.md proactive governance clause correctly limits its growth, and the pattern should not be replicated in other zones without an equally explicit architectural justification.

All four zones in this batch have zero coupling to production code zones, which is architecturally correct — test and script zones should never be imported by production packages, and the import graph confirms this holds

The test coverage strategy is three-tiered and well-separated: web-integration-tests covers web package internals, cross-package-integration-tests covers seam contracts, and monorepo-e2e-tests covers full CLI behavior — each tier has a distinct scope with no overlap

The monorepo-scripts zone breaks the pattern of other zones by having zero cohesion — this is expected for a scripts/ directory but is worth monitoring; if more scripts are added, consider whether they belong in a package-specific scripts/ folder instead

Required tests identified in CLAUDE.md (cli-dev.test.js, scheduler-startup.test.js) are distributed across both the monorepo-e2e-tests and cross-package-integration-tests zones respectively — documentation and zone placement are aligned, reducing risk of accidental exclusion

The four-tier architecture (orchestration → execution → domain → foundation) is correctly reflected in cross-package coupling: none of the 5 zones import from higher tiers, and llm-client imports from nothing — the hierarchy constraint is structurally enforced.

The largest packages (rex at 249 files, hench at 158 files) both have perfect package-level cohesion but documented internal zone fragility. Package-level metrics mask internal structural risks — the governance policies in CLAUDE.md are the critical compensating control, not the cohesion numbers.

All significant cross-zone traffic is concentrated in the web family (web-viewer ↔ web-server dominating with 88+5 imports) — the domain packages (rex, hench, llm-client) are correctly isolated from the web layer's internal hub complexity.

Two satellite micro-zones (sourcevision-completion-reader, sourcevision-prd-resolver) were split from sourcevision-core by community detection — both have zero coupling to the core zone, indicating their source files may lack the internal imports needed to maintain graph connectivity, which is a cohesion fragmentation risk worth investigating.

The sourcevision package's 163-file count makes it the second-largest zone in the monorepo after web-viewer (305 files); unlike web-viewer which has documented internal sub-zone governance, sourcevision-core has no equivalent internal boundary policy — consider establishing one before the zone grows further.

Fixture zone fragmentation (user, json, router all representing the same Go fixture set) adds noise to the zone map; consolidating them under Go Analysis Test Fixtures would give a more accurate picture of sourcevision's test coverage surface.
Four of the five zones in this batch achieve cohesion ≥ 0.6, reflecting the web package's mature internal boundary discipline enforced by boundary-check.test.ts and domain-isolation.test.js.

mcp-parallel-mode is the sole dual-fragility zone in this batch (cohesion 0.33, coupling 0.67); its 1-production-to-4-test-file ratio is a strong signal that community detection grouped it artificially and it should be dissolved into web-server.

The web-server ↔ web-viewer bidirectional import relationship (88 viewer→server, 5 server→viewer) is the primary architectural risk surface in the web package; the gateway pattern contains it effectively today, but both directions should be included in any future zone health regression check.

The complete absence of cross-zone imports involving test zones confirms the monorepo correctly isolates test infrastructure from production code — no test utilities have leaked into production import paths.

The e2e zone (24 files) is disproportionately larger than the integration zone (5 files), reflecting that CLI contract validation has grown faster than seam-level contract testing — consider whether any e2e tests that primarily verify gateway or zone contracts should migrate to the integration zone for faster feedback.

Three of four zones in this batch are pure test zones (zero coupling, high cohesion) — this confirms the test infrastructure follows the same zone hygiene principles as production zones, a positive architectural signal.

packages/web/tests/unit/server/ contains files from 6 zones (mcp-parallel-mode, rex-prd-management, sourcevision-core, viewer-message-pipeline, web-dashboard-ui, web-server-backend) — consider grouping into subdirectories to clarify architectural boundaries
File "packages/hench/tests/e2e/cli-init.test.ts" has 100% of import neighbors in packages/hench/src/schema/ but lives in packages/hench/tests/e2e/ — consider relocating
[call graph] 158 calls: "web-dashboard-ui" → "web-server-backend"
[call graph] 20 calls: "viewer-message-pipeline" → "web-server-backend"
[call graph] 9 calls: "web-dashboard-ui" → "viewer-message-pipeline"
[call graph] 6 calls: "mcp-parallel-mode" → "web-server-backend"

findings.text: Isolated files — no import edges between 3 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Cohesion of 0 is expected and correct — the three shim files (hench.js, rex.js, sourcevision.js) are parallel entry points with no shared logic, so zero intra-zone imports is the correct design.

These shims enable the ndx, rex, hench, and sourcevision CLI aliases — any structural change here should be validated against the full set of command aliases documented in CLAUDE.md to avoid breaking the sv alias.

Zero coupling confirms the bin layer has no upstream runtime dependencies, making it a clean and stable delegation boundary — changes to package CLIs do not require shim updates unless the binary path changes.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Five files covering five distinct integration surfaces is lean and intentional. Resist adding behavior tests here — this zone should remain focused on contract and boundary verification only.

Having separate gateway pipeline tests for the generic case (gateway-pipeline.test.js) and the hench-rex specific case (hench-rex-gateway-pipeline.test.js) is good — it isolates failures and makes it clear when a generic gateway rule vs. a hench-specific contract is broken.
Bidirectional coupling: "web-dashboard-ui" ↔ "web-server-backend" (88+5 crossings) — consider extracting shared interface
Size imbalance: largest zone has 305 files vs smallest with 2 — uneven decomposition

Fan-in hotspot: packages/rex/src/schema/index.ts receives calls from 24 files — high-impact module, changes may have wide ripple effects
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
The 5-file fixture set (Makefile, go.mod, go.sum, one internal file, one vendor file) is an appropriately minimal representative sample for Go project analysis testing.
Zero coupling and cohesion of 1.0 are expected artifacts of an isolated fixture directory, not design quality signals — these metrics are not meaningful at this scale.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

At 158 files, hench is the second-largest zone in the monorepo. Boundary assertions for internal sub-zones (agent/, tools/, prd/, brief/, process/) should be added to the test suite before the zone reaches web-viewer scale, as CLAUDE.md notes this is a pending governance gap.

Cohesion 1.0 and coupling 0.0 at the package level is architecturally ideal for the execution tier — hench sits directly above the domain tier and correctly avoids importing from web or other non-foundation packages.

The dual-gateway design (rex-gateway.ts re-exporting 19 functions + 6 types, llm-gateway.ts re-exporting 30 functions + 10 types) is a strong encapsulation pattern — new cross-package dependencies must be routed through these gateways, not added as ad-hoc leaf-file imports.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

The @n-dx/llm-client scoped package name signals internal-only status — consumers should import exclusively through the public API (src/public.ts) rather than using the subpath escape hatch to reach internal modules.

The coexistence of api-provider.ts and cli-provider.ts abstracts over direct API calls and CLI subprocess invocations — this seam prevents hench from hardcoding execution strategies and enables future provider additions without hench changes.

Zero external coupling is the defining characteristic of a foundation tier — llm-client correctly has no runtime dependencies on rex, hench, sourcevision, or web, preserving the unidirectional dependency invariant.
High coupling (0.67) — 5 imports target "web-server-backend"
Low cohesion (0.33) — files are loosely related, consider splitting this zone

Cohesion of 0.33 and coupling of 0.67 meet both dual-fragility thresholds; with only 1 production file and 4 test files, the zone is almost certainly an artifact of community detection rather than a meaningful architectural boundary.

mcp-session-scoping.test.ts and memory-leak-fixes.test.ts test server-level concerns unrelated to parallel-mode.ts; their presence in this zone creates misleading zone semantics that will confuse future contributors.

routes-mcp.ts (the entry point) is classified in web-server while its test routes-mcp.test.ts lives here — this source/test zone split breaks the convention that tests travel with their production files and should be resolved.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
The algorithmicId suffix '-2' is a community-detection artifact; renaming to a descriptive id prevents tooling from treating it as a secondary fragment of the Sourcevision Analysis Engine zone.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

24 e2e test files with perfect cohesion suggests strong internal organization; as the suite grows, consider grouping by domain (cli/, architecture/, mcp/) using subdirectories to keep navigation tractable.

The presence of architecture-policy.test.js and domain-isolation.test.js in e2e (rather than unit tests) confirms these are spawn-based structural validators — this is intentional and correct per CLAUDE.md's required test list.
cli-dev.test.js is marked a required test in CLAUDE.md — its presence in this zone ensures it runs as part of the standard e2e sweep, reducing the risk of it being skipped.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Cohesion of 1.0 reflects that root files form a densely connected cluster, not that they are semantically uniform — this zone contains CI pipelines, orchestrators, brand utilities, and project metadata side by side.
The spawn-only rule for the orchestration tier is upheld: ci.js, cli.js, and web.js are entry points that delegate to package CLIs rather than importing domain packages directly at runtime.

config.js is documented as the only spawn-exempt exception because cross-package config reads require direct file access — this deliberate tier boundary violation should remain isolated to config.js and not propagate to other orchestration scripts.
Isolated files — no import edges between 2 files, cohesion is unmeasurable (reported as 0)
Neither script has a corresponding test. As maintenance scripts they carry risk of silent breakage — consider adding a smoke test or CI check that validates each script exits cleanly.
Zero cohesion (no internal imports between the two files) is expected here — these are independent scripts, not a module. No action needed on the metric itself.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Cohesion of 1.0 across 11 test files indicates strong thematic unity; all tests share a common CLI-invocation pattern without utility drift.
Zero coupling to any other zone confirms the subprocess invocation pattern is correctly followed — e2e tests exercise the public binary interface, not internal imports.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Rex correctly sits at the domain tier with zero imports from hench, web, or other domain packages — the independence invariant is maintained, allowing rex to be evolved without cascading changes to the autonomous agent or dashboard layers.

The cross-zone import 'rex → web-server: 1 import' visible in the broader codebase is correctly mediated through web/src/server/rex-gateway.ts — this is a gateway re-export, not a leaf-file direct import, confirming the boundary is respected.

The rex-cli internal zone (cohesion 0.25, coupling 0.75) is a dual-fragility zone documented in CLAUDE.md — new CLI command files must be grouped into subdirectories under packages/rex/src/cli/commands/ per the rex-satellite zone policy, not added flat.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
A 2-file zone below the 5-file reliability threshold — cohesion of 1.0 reflects the self-contained test pairing, not genuine internal coherence across multiple collaborating files.
Detachment from sourcevision-core suggests completion-reader.ts may be a standalone utility with no imports from the broader analyzer graph; verify it uses shared types to avoid silent duplication.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
Cohesion of 1.0 across 163 files is exceptional and indicates strong internal coherence — all files share a common analysis domain without unrelated concerns bleeding in.
Consumed exclusively via domain-gateway.ts in the web package, correctly implementing the gateway pattern and keeping the dependency surface auditable.

Two detached micro-zones (completion-reader, prd-epic-resolver) suggest isolated files with no imports to the main analyzer graph — if confirmed, these represent cohesion fragmentation candidates for re-integration.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
A 2-file micro-zone below the 5-file reliability threshold; cohesion of 1.0 reflects only the test-pairing pattern, not broader internal coherence.

Cross-package domain reference in the file name (prd-epic-resolver in the sourcevision package) warrants a review of whether this command imports from rex — if so, it must route through a gateway or be relocated.
9 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
High cohesion (0.98) — files are tightly interconnected

88 imports from web-viewer to web-server is the largest cross-zone edge in the web package; periodically audit these to confirm they remain type imports or gateway re-exports and have not promoted to runtime server dependencies.
Cohesion of 0.98 across 305 files is exceptional and confirms the viewer forms a well-bounded architectural unit; the import-graph community detection result validates the gateway isolation strategy.

The bidirectional edge with web-server (88 viewer→server, 5 server→viewer) is the primary coupling surface risk; the 5 server→viewer imports warrant review to confirm they do not import Preact or browser globals into the Express runtime.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

Both test files serve as policy enforcement gates (zone boundaries and build contracts) rather than behavior tests — this is a valuable architectural pattern that should be expanded to other high-risk zones.

With only 2 files and perfect cohesion, this zone is well-scoped. If web package integration tests grow significantly, consider splitting boundary tests from build-contract tests into separate describe blocks or files.
High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected

No entry points are registered in the zone graph, which likely means the landing page is served as a static asset — document the serving path explicitly so contributors do not accidentally import it as a module.
Zero coupling to any other zone is the cleanest isolation boundary in the codebase; the 2-file size means metrics are indicative rather than authoritative, but the pattern is correct.
High cohesion (0.87) — files are tightly interconnected
Cohesion of 0.87 with only 0.13 outward coupling is a healthy server layer — the gateway pattern for rex and sourcevision is successfully bounding the cross-package import surface.

Rex and sourcevision each have exactly 1 import edge into web-server, consistent with their MCP server factory registrations flowing through domain-gateway.ts and rex-gateway.ts per the documented gateway rules.

The injection seam registry entry for register-scheduler.ts is architecturally sound; ensure the RegisterSchedulerOptions interface remains the sole named type governing this seam to make future refactoring safe.
Zone "crash-recovery-ui" has files across 5 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory

Bidirectional imports between crash and web-viewer (2 each direction) are documented in CLAUDE.md as an intentional architectural pattern; the direct crash→web-shared bypass must remain the sole exception to the viewer gateway rule.

Cohesion at 0.6 sits just above the dual-fragility threshold (0.5); CLAUDE.md designates this zone for proactive governance — apply the two-consumer rule to any new additions before cohesion can degrade below the threshold.
crash-detector-test-support.ts in tests/helpers is a good pattern for test infrastructure isolation; it keeps the production crash zone boundary clean while still providing shared test utilities.

Cohesion 1 and coupling 0 confirm correct fixture isolation; the zone detection splitting this from the existing Go Analysis Test Fixtures zone is an artifact of community detection on a small, disconnected subgraph.

Community detection split this Go fixture into a separate zone from the user and json fixtures despite all three being part of the same test fixture set; merging all three into Go Analysis Test Fixtures is safe.
This zone and the user zone are both fragments of the same go-project fixture; they should be merged into a single Go Analysis Test Fixtures zone to reduce map noise.

Cohesion of 0.64 is acceptable for a zone this size, but inclusion of build config and unrelated utilities (worktree-validation, token-usage constants) in the detected cluster indicates the zone boundary is slightly over-broad.

The use-facet-state.ts hook and token-usage.ts view appearing in this zone alongside core messaging middleware suggests community detection is pulling in neighboring files with shared import targets; verify these files import through messaging/index.ts rather than leaf files.
God function: ExplorerView in packages/web/src/viewer/views/sv-explorer.ts calls 103 unique functions — consider decomposing into smaller, focused functions
Tightly coupled modules: packages/web/src/server/routes-hench.ts and packages/web/src/server/types.ts — 79 cross-file calls (unidirectional). Consider extracting shared interface or merging
